topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

My phone buzzed with a notification. A profile picture flashed onto the screen—a grainy, slightly distorted photo of a kid wearing a faded band t-shirt, grinning with a smirk that used to make my stomach clench. The name attached was 'Jax.' My breath hitched. Jax. The name felt like a physical blow, a sudden, unwelcome shift in the air. I stared at the request, my thumb hovering over the 'Accept' button, my fingers suddenly clumsy. A strange knot tightened in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. Why now? After all this time, after everything? I quickly minimized the message, shoving the phone face down on the desk, the silence of the room suddenly deafening. I started pacing the small space between my chair and the window, running a hand through my hair, trying to physically shake off the unwelcome jolt of memory.

[story 2]

I picked up my phone, and the screen illuminated my face. A notification glowed: 'Jax has sent you a friend request.' My jaw went slack. It was him. The kid who used to find every awkward moment I had and twist them into something humiliating. A wave of heat washed over my cheeks, a flush that started at my neck and spread upward. I reread the request three times, my eyes darting back and forth across the screen as if expecting the image to change or the message to morph into something entirely different. My hands started trembling, and I had to grip the edge of the table to steady myself. I leaned back in my chair, the wood groaning softly under my weight, and let out a slow, shaky exhale. What was the logic behind this sudden outreach? It made no sense at all.

[story 3]

The chime of the incoming notification was sharp, cutting through the mundane rhythm of my afternoon. I glanced down, and the familiar, infuriating profile picture materialized. Jax. The one whose shadow used to loom over every hallway I walked. I felt a distinct tightening in my throat, a sensation like trying to swallow sandpaper. I didn't move immediately; I just sat there, frozen, my gaze locked onto the screen. My mind raced, cycling through years of shared, painful history, trying to find a logical thread to connect this digital intrusion to my current reality. Slowly, deliberately, I tapped the notification, my movements stiff and uncertain, as if navigating a field of invisible, sharp obstacles. A deep furrow formed between my brows as I processed the sheer unexpectedness of the gesture.
